text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Malhotra
Riverside Medical Centre

12 Wharf Street, London, SE8 3AB
Dear Dr Priya Malhotra
Michael Turner, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: H829374 NHS No. 7492013568
Flat 4, 28 Millbank Court, London, SW1P 3GE 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. Since my last assessment in February 2025, there has been a clear deterioration in his seizure control, which he relates in part to frequent travel for work with occasional missed doses of medication. He described increasing episodes that often cluster at the end of long working days and, notably, he has noticed a pattern of attacks occurring after extended periods of intensive laptop and smartphone use. He characterises these as brief staring spells with loss of awareness on several occasions each week, alongside two recent collapses with limb-jerking in the past six weeks. His wife corroborates a rise in frequency and a more prolonged recovery after the recent convulsive events.

He is currently prescribed Levetiracetam 1500 mg b.d., Sodium Valproate 500 mg t.d.s., Lamotrigine 100 mg b.d., and Clobazam 10 mg nocte. He reports generally tolerating these, though he has had increasing daytime fatigue and intermittent difficulty with concentration. No new rashes or focal neurological symptoms have been reported. He denies alcohol excess but confirms irregular sleep during travel, time-zone shifts, and occasional late-night work deadlines. He carries a medication supply but admits to missed doses on flight days.

On examination today, cardiorespiratory and general examination were unremarkable. Neurological examination was non-focal. Weight stable. BP 128/76 mmHg. There were no signs of drug toxicity clinically.

Given the worsening seizure burden, the temporal association with extended device use, and the acknowledged adherence lapses during frequent trips, I have advised a structured adherence plan coordinated with his travel schedule, the use of electronic reminders, and carrying duplicate supplies to avoid missed doses. I discussed seizure first aid and reinforced safety measures regarding bathing, cooking, and heights. He has been issued a seizure diary to document timing, duration, potential precipitating factors, and recovery, with particular note of episodes following prolonged periods of screen engagement.

Investigations: I have requested an expedited ambulatory EEG with video, along with serum drug levels for Valproate and Lamotrigine, full blood count, U&E, LFTs, and vitamin D. I have also arranged an MRI brain with epilepsy protocol to reassess structural causes as it has been over five years since his last imaging.

Plan: Pending results, we will review urgently in six weeks. If levels suggest subtherapeutic exposure, we will consider dose optimisation. If events persist despite improved adherence, escalation of therapy or rationalisation of the current regimen will be considered. I have provided information on local and national epilepsy support services and offered referral to our epilepsy specialist nurse for adherence counselling. He does not currently drive and understands the requirement to inform the DVLA in the context of recent events.

In summary, there is a recent and clinically significant decline in seizure control, likely multifactorial, with frequent travel and occasional missed doses being important contributors. The reported pattern of attacks occurring after long stretches of intense device usage has been noted and will be explored further through ambulatory EEG and structured monitoring. I will keep you updated following investigations and the forthcoming review.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: brief staring spells with loss of awareness on several occasions each week

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states the patient has ongoing seizures with increased frequency. It explicitly mentions "brief staring spells with loss of awareness on several occasions each week" and also "two recent collapses with limb-jerking in the past six weeks." Converting the latter: 2 in 6 weeks ≈ 1 per 3 weeks. Applying the rule to choose the highest frequency, the weekly staring spells are more frequent than the convulsive events. The exact number per week is vague ("several"), so use 'multiple' per week per formatting rules.
seizure_frequency_number: brief staring spells with loss of awareness on several occasions each week

flaws.description: Gold (multiple per week) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
